---
title: "Prototype Roadmap"
chapter_id: "prototype-roadmap"
part_id: "evidence-implementation-living-book"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-27"
primary_sources:
  - "viea"
  - "benchmaxxing"
  - "scf"
  - "vcm_public"
  - "planforge"
  - "beastbrain"
  - "beastbrain_timeless"
  - "bugbrain"
  - "moecot"
  - "moecot_md"
  - "road_to_agi"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "project_theseus_whitepaper"
  - "theseus_plan_compiler"
  - "theseus_self_evolution_system"
  - "theseus_architecture_gate"
  - "theseus_operator_os"
  - "theseus_circle_transfer"
  - "circle_ai_contract_suite"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Appendix C now records passage-reviewed mappings for all nineteen assigned sources, including source-note-only connector/recovery sources and pinned local Project Theseus/Circle references; support remains argument until phase completion artifacts, accepted gates, reproduced checks, or accepted evidence transitions justify promotion."
  - "The prototype_phase_record fixture and AsiStackProofs.PrototypeRoadmap finite-record predicates validate record shape and gate predicates only; no phase completion, phase acceptance, dependency-gate audit, phase execution, or roadmap milestone is promoted."
  - "BeastBrain and BugBrain are lineage and cautionary sources only here; source-reported hardware, performance, context, AGI, consciousness, security, autonomy, and cost claims are not promoted."
  - "No support-state promotion, benchmark result, model-quality result, deployment/readiness claim, phase-acceptance result, or raw source publication is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `prototype-roadmap` |
| Part | Part IV - Evidence, Implementation, and the Living Book |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-27 |
| Primary source records | `viea`, `benchmaxxing`, `scf`, `vcm_public`, `planforge`, `beastbrain`, `beastbrain_timeless`, `bugbrain`, `moecot`, `moecot_md`, `road_to_agi`, `coherence_exchange`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `theseus_plan_compiler`, `theseus_self_evolution_system`, `theseus_architecture_gate`, `theseus_operator_os`, `theseus_circle_transfer`, `circle_ai_contract_suite` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `viea`, `benchmaxxing`, `scf`, `vcm_public`, `planforge`, `beastbrain`; supporting: `beastbrain_timeless`, `bugbrain`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `theseus_plan_compiler`, `theseus_self_evolution_system`, `theseus_architecture_gate`, `theseus_operator_os`, `theseus_circle_transfer`, `circle_ai_contract_suite`; connector/recovery: `moecot`, `moecot_md`, `road_to_agi`, `coherence_exchange` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `viea`, `benchmaxxing`, `scf`, `vcm_public`, `planforge`, `beastbrain`, `beastbrain_timeless`, `bugbrain`, `moecot`, `moecot_md`, `road_to_agi`, `coherence_exchange`, `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `theseus_plan_compiler`, `theseus_self_evolution_system`, `theseus_architecture_gate`, `theseus_operator_os`, `theseus_circle_transfer`, `circle_ai_contract_suite`; raw cache: `viea`, `benchmaxxing`, `scf`, `vcm_public`, `planforge`, `beastbrain`, `beastbrain_timeless`, `bugbrain`, `moecot_md`; source-note-only or connector/recovery: `moecot`, `road_to_agi`, `coherence_exchange`; pinned local references: Project Theseus at `1ad88a22`, Circle Math at `63b0f511` |
| Test state | `prototype_phase_record.valid.json` passes repository-level protocol fixture validation; `AsiStackProofs.PrototypeRoadmap` builds finite-record predicates over phase unlock and claim-promotion reviews. Phase acceptance, dependency-gate, execution, benchmark, and evidence-state audits remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This roadmap is sequencing logic, not evidence. A phase milestone, source-reported result, or local render success cannot promote a capability claim without the relevant artifacts.

The previous chapter shows how report-first implementation references can guide the stack without becoming public empirical proof. This chapter turns that caution into build order. The prototype should earn later agency by first making source ingestion, artifact continuity, claim ledgers, validation, and authority stops boringly reliable.

The roadmap is therefore not a schedule. It is a dependency contract for trust. A phase is allowed to unlock later work only when the artifacts that would make that later work inspectable already exist.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

The Theseus chapter gives implementation pressure. This chapter turns that pressure into build order. The roadmap starts with evidence hygiene, source ingestion, artifact continuity, and claim discipline before it asks for stronger agency.

For a human reader, the roadmap is a humility device. A phase is not a result, and a milestone is not evidence. The build should earn later autonomy by first proving that the project can preserve sources, traces, tests, proofs, residuals, and release records.
:::

## Problem

The ASI Stack needs a build order that does not create agency before auditability, evidence, and authority controls exist. A prototype that begins with autonomous self-improvement skips the machinery that would make improvement inspectable.

The build order must also prevent partial demos from becoming institutional facts. A useful local trace, agent loop, retrieval store, or benchmark dashboard can guide engineering, but it should not become a capability claim until phase acceptance records say what was run, what passed, what failed, what remains blocked, and what the milestone does not imply.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

A roadmap that jumps to autonomous improvement skips source matrices, artifact graphs, claim ledgers, tests, and authority controls. It also makes useful prototypes dangerous: once something feels helpful, teams are tempted to skip evidence discipline.

VIEA says durable artifacts first. SCF says replacement and improvement need stable fields. VCM says context admission and adequacy must be visible. PlanForge says plans need typed contracts. Project Theseus says report-first local machinery should gate training and self-evolution.

Prototype roadmaps often hide dependency direction. They list features in an attractive order, but do not say which earlier gates make later agency legitimate. The ASI Stack needs the opposite: every attractive capability should point backward to the evidence, authority, replay, and rollback infrastructure it depends on.

## Core Claim

[prototype-roadmap.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] The prototype should grow in staged increments from source matrix and artifact graph to governed capability replacement.

The claim remains at `argument` support. The source notes support a staged build sequence, but this chapter does not claim that the prototype phases have been completed.

No roadmap phase has been accepted, executed, or used to promote a capability claim in this repository.

The roadmap should therefore be read less like a product plan and more like a dependency graph for trust. The early phases create the conditions under which later phases can mean anything: sources can be inspected, artifacts can be found again, claims can be downgraded, and authority checks can stop an unsafe action. Later phases are only legitimate when they inherit those conditions. A planner that cannot preserve source provenance is not ready to dispatch work. A runtime that cannot replay actions is not ready to close loops. A replacement system that cannot roll back is not ready to improve itself.

This ordering also protects useful prototypes from their own momentum. A half-working local agent, benchmark harness, context memory, or route scheduler can feel convincing long before it is governable. The phase record slows that down. It says which artifacts exist, which checks passed, which blockers remain, and which claims are still forbidden. That makes the roadmap a pressure surface for better engineering rather than a story that later work was inevitable.

Each phase should have entry criteria and exit criteria. Entry criteria prevent starting a phase when its prerequisites are absent. Exit criteria prevent treating a phase as accepted because it produced an impressive artifact. A phase can produce useful work while still failing exit.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now records nineteen passage-reviewed mappings for this claim. The review sharpens what each source may support while keeping every non-claim visible.

| Source group | Reviewed support | Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| Core stack sequencing sources: `viea`, `benchmaxxing`, `scf`, `vcm_public`, `planforge` | Durable artifacts, command contracts, claim ledgers, benchmark ratchets, stable fields, governed context, typed plans, gates, and residual discipline before higher-agency phases. | No phase acceptance, prototype execution, benchmark result, dependency-gate audit, or claim promotion. |
| Prototype lineage sources: `beastbrain`, `beastbrain_timeless`, `bugbrain` | Local, stateful, resource-constrained, memory/planning/verification/routing vocabulary and cautionary prototype lineage. | No hardware, performance, context-length, security, autonomy, cost, AGI, consciousness, build, emulation, or hardware-test claim. |
| Connector/recovery runtime sources: `moecot`, `moecot_md`, `road_to_agi`, `coherence_exchange` | Runtime-reference context, release-wording normalization, remaining-work/blocker categories, governance/evidence-interface vocabulary, and source-reported status boundaries. | Source-note-only or variant use; no runtime artifact, reproduced command, benchmark evidence, economic mechanism, or roadmap gate. |
| Theseus implementation references: `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `theseus_plan_compiler`, `theseus_self_evolution_system`, `theseus_architecture_gate`, `theseus_operator_os`, `theseus_circle_transfer` | Report-first implementation lane, plan compiler contracts, self-evolution gates, architecture-gate checks, operator/work-board controls, and structural transfer design. | No current report bundle, compiler command, gate output, dashboard run, board step, transfer consumer, benchmark result, model-quality result, or deployment readiness. |
| Circle contracts and formal/fixture artifacts: `circle_ai_contract_suite`, `prototype_phase_record.valid.json`, `AsiStackProofs.PrototypeRoadmap` | Future proof-carrying contract milestone shape and finite record-level phase-gate predicates. | No Circle command, generated contract pack, acceptance check, external Circle Lean build, receipt replay, downstream consumer, or semantic proof of phase completion. |

## Mechanism

The roadmap is a sequence of gated records, not a promise that the later stages already work. VIEA argues for durable artifacts before execution, Benchmaxxing argues for regression floors before frontier claims, VCM argues for source-bound context before memory-heavy work, PlanForge argues for typed plans before dispatch, and SCF argues for governed replacement before improvement. The Project Theseus sources add a report-first implementation discipline: every phase should leave enough reports, gates, residuals, and work-board state for the next phase to inspect.

Each Prototype Phase Record therefore answers four questions: what becomes possible, which artifacts must exist first, which checks can block promotion, and which non-claims prevent a roadmap milestone from masquerading as evidence.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Phase proposal"] --> B["Deliverables"]
  B --> C["Acceptance gates"]
  C --> D{"Gates pass?"}
  D -- "yes" --> E["Unlock dependent phase"]
  D -- "no" --> F["Block / residual / revise"]
  E --> G["Release notes + evidence refs"]
  F --> G
```

Recommended phase order:

- Source matrix, source notes, claim ledger, and publication validation.
- Artifact graph, protocol schemas, fixtures, and proof manifest.
- Intent contracts, PlanForge DAGs, VCM context records, and typed jobs.
- Runtime adapters, permissions, audit logs, replay, and procedural memory.
- Routing heads, specialist registry, readiness gates, and benchmark ratchets.
- Stable capability fields, replacement transactions, rollback, and bounded self-improvement.

Self-improvement comes late because it depends on evaluator integrity, rollback, governance rights, evidence ledgers, and a demonstrated ability to preserve residuals instead of hiding them.

The first phase is not glamorous, but it is the root of the whole build. A source matrix, source notes, claim ledger, and publication validator give the system a memory for what it is allowed to say. Without that layer, later automation will optimize fluent architecture prose while silently dropping evidence boundaries. The second phase turns that memory into durable artifacts: schemas, fixtures, proof manifests, release records, and audit logs that can be checked without trusting the latest explanation.

Only after that does the prototype need richer agency. Intent contracts and PlanForge-style DAGs can lower goals into work. VCM records can decide what context is admitted. Typed jobs can describe what an executor is actually being asked to do. Runtime adapters, permissions, replay, and procedural memory then decide which effects may happen and which successful patterns can become reusable tools. Routing, readiness gates, and benchmark ratchets come after these control surfaces because specialists and benchmarks are dangerous when they are not tied to authority, evidence, and rollback.

The final phases are capability replacement and bounded self-improvement. They require stable capability fields, acceptance gates, regression floors, evaluator integrity, rollback windows, governance rights, and residual accounting. The roadmap does not say that self-improvement is impossible before those pieces exist. It says that before those pieces exist, self-improvement is not an evidence-governed engineering operation in the sense this book cares about.

The roadmap should explicitly carry phase debt. If a phase proceeds with a missing artifact, manual workaround, unreviewed source, or provisional gate, the debt must be recorded with an owner boundary and retirement condition. Otherwise prototypes accumulate hidden assumptions that later phases treat as infrastructure.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Prototype Phase Record.

Minimum fields:

- `phase_id`
- `phase_name`
- `unlocks`
- `required_artifacts`
- `acceptance_gates`
- `blocked_by`
- `validation_commands`
- `evidence_refs`
- `non_claims`
- `status`

Each phase unlocks later layers only after acceptance gates pass. Changelogs and releases document what actually changed. Benchmark and proof chapters decide what the phase is allowed to claim.

The interface should also record dependency edges. A runtime-adapter phase depends on typed jobs, approval receipts, and audit logs. A procedural-memory phase depends on artifact graphs and regression checks. A self-improvement phase depends on SCFs, rollback, protected invariants, evidence ledgers, and independent evaluators. These edges are the roadmap's control surface.

## Invariants

- Phases have acceptance criteria.
- Self-improvement is gated.
- Roadmap milestones are not test results.
- Reported external readiness is not local reproduction.
- Agency does not precede auditability.

Another invariant is no irreversible unlock from provisional evidence. A phase can allow research use before full acceptance, but it cannot unlock default execution, promotion, self-improvement, or public capability claims while its required gates remain provisional.

## Failure modes

- Building agency before auditability.
- Skipping verification because prototypes feel useful.
- Treating roadmap as evidence.
- Letting operator convenience weaken safety gates.
- Losing remaining-work items from source-reported roadmaps.

The subtle failure is prototype theater. The project shows a board, dashboard, demo, or generated report that looks like an operating system, while the acceptance gates, replay artifacts, and evidence transitions behind it are missing. The roadmap should make that visible by separating demo output from phase acceptance.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The roadmap becomes executable through a prototype phase schema and fixture. The current fixture records deliverables, gates, blockers, commands, evidence references, non-claims, and status. It does not mean the named phase is complete or that dependent prototypes are unlocked.

The next useful implementation step is a dependency-gate fixture set: one phase blocked by missing source notes, one phase allowed for research but not promotion, one phase with recorded phase debt and retirement condition, and one self-improvement phase blocked because evaluator independence is absent. That would test roadmap semantics without claiming phase completion.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The logical end state is a phase-gated build controller for the ASI Stack. The roadmap would not be a list of aspirations. It would be a dependency graph that decides which prototypes can be built, which are blocked, which are allowed only as research demos, and which may affect claim support after evidence review.

At that mature boundary, every phase would have explicit prerequisites, acceptance gates, commands, required artifacts, evidence refs, residuals, unlocks, phase debt, retirement criteria, and non-claims. A later phase could not borrow credibility from an earlier milestone unless the acceptance record exists and the downstream dependency matches the evidence boundary.

The mature product surface would include:

- A prototype phase registry spanning source inventory, artifact graph, claim ledger, planning, VCM, typed execution, verification, benchmarks, SCF gates, policy optimization, stewardship, and recursive self-improvement.
- Dependency gates that distinguish "can research," "can demo," "can integrate," "can promote," and "can release."
- Acceptance packets with commands, environment notes, artifacts, proof/spec receipts, benchmark ratchets, reviewer refs, residuals, and support-state effects.
- Phase-debt ledgers for shortcuts, missing sources, missing validators, incomplete tests, unsupported claims, and private artifacts.
- Evaluator-independence gates before readiness promotion, policy learning, capability replacement, or recursive self-improvement.
- Failure closure for prototype theater, skipped verification, roadmap-as-evidence, operator convenience weakening gates, and lost remaining-work items.

In that final product, a builder could ask the roadmap what to implement next and receive a precise answer: the smallest allowed slice, the evidence needed to advance, and the blocked claims that must not move. The phase-gated build controller remains a target architecture until real phase packets, dependency-gate checks, acceptance reviews, and evidence transitions show that roadmap progress changes implementation state without laundering claim strength.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Prototype phase record fixture validation | Validate that a phase record names unlocks, required artifacts, acceptance gates, blockers, validation commands, evidence refs, non-claims, and status. | implemented; passing via `python3 scripts/validate_protocol_examples.py` |
| Phase acceptance checklist | Check that dependent phases have gates and artifacts. | planned; not run |
| Dependency gate review | Check that self-improvement depends on evidence, rollback, and evaluator integrity. | planned; not run |
| Prototype evidence-state audit | Check that roadmap milestones do not promote claims by themselves. | planned; not run |

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:roadmap.phases.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.PrototypeRoadmap` | A roadmap phase can unlock a dependent phase only after acceptance gates pass. | implemented |
| `lean:roadmap.phases.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.PrototypeRoadmap` | A phase milestone cannot promote a claim without evidence artifacts. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over phase unlock and claim-promotion reviews. They prove only the record-level gates: dependent phases require declared passing acceptance gates, and a milestone cannot promote a claim without evidence artifacts. They do not prove that any prototype phase is complete, that dependent prototypes are safe to run, or that any capability claim has earned stronger support.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Intent, artifacts, routing, verification, runtime, feedback, and residuals. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `benchmaxxing` | Benchmaxxing: The Performance Ratchet | benchmarks_evidence | Benchmark frontier, regression, and anti-Goodhart discipline. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `scf` | Stable Capability Fields | governance_recursive_self_improvement | Stable replacement and rollback substrate. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `vcm_public` | Virtual_Context_Memory_v1 | memory_context | Governed context compiler and adequacy/admission separation. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `planforge` | PlanForge | planning_control | Goal-to-execution compilation, scheduling, and intelligence arbitrage. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `beastbrain` | BeastBrain Cognitive Architecture | whole_stack_lineage | Local, stateful stack vocabulary and implementation-roadmap lineage; source-reported hardware/performance/cost claims not promoted. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `beastbrain_timeless` | BeastBrain Timeless Edition | whole_stack_lineage | Evergreen local-memory, planning, verification, and hardware-adaptation framing; no milestone validation. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `bugbrain` | BugBrain | edge_efficiency_lineage | Resource-constrained prototype lineage; build/emulation/hardware/AGI/consciousness claims not promoted. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Runtime/reference implementation context, readiness gates, ledgers, replay, and blockers; not reproduced here. | source note available; connector/recovery required |
| `moecot_md` | MoECOT Markdown Export | implementation_reference_variant | Terminology and release-wording normalization; not independent corroboration. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `road_to_agi` | Road To AGI | strategic_roadmap | Remaining-work, blocker, and source-reported status context; commands/results not reproduced. | source note available; auth-gate/source-note-only placeholder |
| `coherence_exchange` | Coherence Exchange | governance_evidence_interface | Optional governance/evidence-interface vocabulary and contestability framing; speculative economics not promoted. | source note available; connector/recovery required |
| `project_theseus_whitepaper` | Project Theseus Whitepaper | report_first_rmi_prototype | Report-first local RMI implementation reference and safety/report boundary. | pinned local project reference |
| `theseus_plan_compiler` | Theseus Plan Compiler | planning_control | Goal contracts, semantic IR DAGs, VCM slices, routes, contract hashes, and replay-trace targets. | pinned local project reference |
| `theseus_self_evolution_system` | Theseus Self-Evolution System | recursive_self_improvement_governance | Evidence-first self-evolution lane, intervention ladder, ATTD gates, and outcome ledgers. | pinned local project reference |
| `theseus_architecture_gate` | Theseus Architecture Gate | readiness_gate_governance | Gate checks before heavy training; source-reported snapshot not rerun here. | pinned local project reference |
| `theseus_operator_os` | Hive Operator OS and Work Board | labor_os_operator_surface | Durable work board, operator controls, node registry, TTLs, kill switches, and feedback routing. | pinned local project reference |
| `theseus_circle_transfer` | Circle Calculus Transfer Lane | proof_carrying_ai_contracts | Structural transfer design for future private experiments; quality/runtime/model-improvement claims not promoted. | pinned local project reference |
| `circle_ai_contract_suite` | Circle Calculus AI Contract Suite | proof_carrying_ai_contracts | Contract packs, theorem-linked fields, receipt boundaries, and non-claim discipline. | pinned local project reference |

## Summary

The prototype should grow as evidence infrastructure grows: source matrix, claim ledger, artifact graph, schemas, plans, context, typed jobs, runtime adapters, routing, readiness, benchmarks, SCFs, and only then bounded self-improvement.

That order is intentionally conservative. It asks the project to earn agency by first proving that it can remember sources, preserve artifacts, admit failures, replay checks, and refuse unsupported promotions. The next chapter applies the same rule to the manuscript itself.

The practical standard is that every later capability should be able to point to the earlier records that made it safe to exist. If the pointer is missing, the roadmap should block, narrow, or mark the capability as research-only.
